scenario: Forge Government tracks 4000 records per week on Camunda 8.8 Forms. Each node carries a property literally named parent, and the template loops over 25 nodes while also needing to read a root property on the surrounding scope, without the accessor colliding with the node's own parent field within 200 ms per render.
question: Which Feelers syntax avoids the collision when iterated data already uses a property literally named parent?

options.text: Renaming the field at the BPMN data input mapping level so the conflicting property is removed from the node objects before the form template is rendered
Using parent.parent inside the loop body so the template skips one level above the node and reaches the surrounding scope above the iterated array
Using the underscore-wrapped form of the parent accessor inside the loop body, which makes the accessor reach the surrounding scope instead of the node's own parent property
Wrapping the loop in an if block and reading the surrounding value through a separate FEEL expression evaluated before the loop opens its iteration body

a.text: Incorrect. The collision is resolved at the template level by the underscore accessor; rewriting the data through input mappings is not the documented mechanism.

b.text: Incorrect. parent.parent would still resolve the inner parent property of the node first, so the collision is not avoided this way.

c.text: Correct. When the data already uses parent or this as a key, the alternative syntax surrounds the accessor with underscores, so _parent_ reaches the surrounding scope.

d.text: Incorrect. Splitting the template into an if-and-loop pattern does not address the conflict on the parent identifier inside the loop body itself.

explanation: Feelers offers an alternative accessor syntax that surrounds parent or this with underscores, so _parent_ and _this_ reach the surrounding scope or current item without colliding with data fields of the same name.
